3A_Bibliographic.citation: Nagata, J. M., Smith, N., Zamora, G., Sajjad, O. M., Ganson, K. T., Testa, A., & Jackson, D. B. (2023). Problematic social media use and alcohol expectancies in early adolescents. BMC Public Health, 23, 430.

3B_Sample_Data.verbatim_sample_description: Data analyzed in this study (Year 2 assessment; 2018-2020; 4.0 release) were collected by adolescents' self-report. Participants with missing data for screen time use and alcohol expectancies data were excluded (Additional File; Table S1), leaving a total sample of 9,008 adolescents.

3C_Exposure.exposure_variable: Social media time (hours/day) and problematic social media use (SMAQ score)
3C_Exposure.exposure_instrument: ABCD Youth Screen Time Survey (social media time); Social Media Addiction Questionnaire (SMAQ)
3C_Exposure.operationalization: Continuous (social media hours/day weighted average; SMAQ mean score 1-6)
3C_Exposure.verbatim_exposure_description: Time spent on social media was collected using adolescents' self-reported hours and minutes on any typical weekday or weekend day they spent visiting 'social media apps (e.g., Snapchat, Facebook, Twitter, Instagram, TikTok, etc.)' from the ABCD Youth Screen Time Survey... The ABCD Study measured problematic social media use using the Social Media Addiction Questionnaire (SMAQ), comprised of six items modeled after the Bergen Facebook Addiction Scale.

3D_Outcome.verbatim_outcome_description: Alcohol expectancies were assessed via adolescents' self-report through the validated Alcohol Expectancy Questionnaire-Adolescent, Brief (AEQ-AB). The AEQ-AB questionnaire is a 7-item instrument... On a 5-point Likert scale ranging from 1 to 5, youth reported how much they agreed/disagreed with statements about the effects of alcohol.

3E_Design_Analysis.model_type_detail: Multiple adjusted linear regression with propensity weights; three models with different exposure combinations
3E_Design_Analysis.covariates_listed: age, sex, race/ethnicity, sexual orientation, household income, parent education, parental marital status, study site
3E_Design_Analysis.verbatim_analysis_description: Adjusted linear regression analyses were conducted to estimate cross-sectional associations between social media use variables and alcohol expectancies (positive and negative), adjusted for age, race/ethnicity, sex, household income, parent education, parent marital status, and study site... Given the large sample size, two-sided alpha was set at p<0.01.
3E_Design_Analysis.notes: Used a stricter alpha of 0.01 given large sample size

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: Social media time (hours per day) - Model 3
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Table 2 Model 3: Social media time (hours per day) B=-0.010 (95% CI -0.022; 0.002), p=0.106

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: Social Media Addiction Questionnaire Score - Model 3
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Table 2 Model 3: Social Media Addiction Questionnaire Score B=0.045 (95% CI 0.020; 0.069), p<0.001

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: Social media time (hours per day) - Model 3
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Table 2 Model 3: Social media time B=0.005 (95% CI -0.010; 0.020), p=0.504

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: Social Media Addiction Questionnaire Score - Model 3
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Table 2 Model 3: Social Media Addiction Questionnaire Score B=0.072 (95% CI 0.043; 0.101), p<0.001

3G_Reporting_Quality.large_n_verbatim: Given the large sample size, two-sided alpha was set at p<0.01.
3G_Reporting_Quality.small_es_verbatim: Given the small effect sizes of the current study, future studies should further examine these relationships prospectively... Because alcohol expectancies are modifiable and linked with alcohol initiation, they could be a target for future prevention efforts.
3G_Reporting_Quality.notes: Paper uses stricter alpha threshold acknowledging large sample, notes small effect sizes, but still issues recommendations for policy action based on modifiable-factor argument.

3H_Recommendations.recommendation_verbatim: Social media platforms could implement stricter rules regarding alcohol advertising targeted at minors. Parents can consider implementing parental controls on devices, enhancing privacy settings on social media, and monitoring their children's social media activity. Healthcare providers can ask patients and families about media use concerns, including problematic social media use as part of routine health maintenance.

3I_Rhetorical_Moves.modifiable_factor_verbatim: Because alcohol expectancies are modifiable and linked with alcohol initiation, they could be a target for future prevention efforts.
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.discussion_characterization: The discussion frames problematic social media use (not mere time on social media) as the key predictor associated with both positive and negative alcohol expectancies, invoking Social Learning Theory as a mechanistic explanation. It acknowledges small effect sizes and cross-sectional limitations but proceeds to issue extensive recommendations for social media platforms, parents, and clinicians. The modifiable-factor framing is used to justify action despite small effects.
